Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I tried to set aside time after my part-time job shifts, but those hours changed every week so nothing was ever consistent. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often spread out my notes on the floor and physically move things around to understand concepts, which took more time than just reading. I aimed for about 15 hours a week, but sometimes life got in the way and I'd have to cram everything into weekends.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening at home. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, studying a bit every day, but then other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was breathing down my neck. With my work schedule being unpredictable and not having a lot of resources to fall back on, I had to be flexible and just grab study time whenever I could find it.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due soon or when I got a notification about a new assignment. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed, since my attendance wasn't great and I worried about falling behind. The discussion posts also pulled me in because I actually enjoyed connecting with other students and seeing different perspectives on the material.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS in the morning became kind of automatic for me, like checking my phone messages. I think it happened because I set it as my browser homepage on my phone, so every time I opened the internet, there it was. It wasn't a deep habit though—it faded when things got stressful and I started avoiding reminders of schoolwork.
Q5: The discussion forums were honestly the most useful for me because I learn better when I can talk things through and hear how other people understand the material. I also really valued any videos that showed practical demonstrations—being a kinesthetic learner, watching someone actually do something helped way more than just reading about it. The 47 minutes or so I spent on videos each session were probably my most productive learning moments.
Q6: I often skipped the long reading PDFs and some of the supplementary articles. With limited data on my phone plan sometimes and just not having a printer at home, those resources felt less accessible to me. I also tended to ignore the optional quizzes because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already stretched thin trying to keep up with required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams, or when it involved actually doing something rather than just passively absorbing information. If I could practice a skill or apply a concept hands-on, I was all in. I also valued activities where I could interact with classmates because those discussions helped solidify my understanding in ways that solo studying couldn't.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't really afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them even when I was exhausted or overwhelmed. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got pushed aside because I was just trying to survive and meet the minimum requirements. It's not that I didn't want to explore more; I just didn't have the bandwidth.
Q9: Oh definitely, a lot of my studying happened completely offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good wifi at the library, then study them later at home without internet. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd spread out and reorganize physically to help me understand. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my kitchen table with printed notes and borrowed books, working through problems by hand for hours. I'd also meet up with a study partner at her place and we'd quiz each other verbally—super helpful but completely invisible online. Sometimes I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times, pausing and rewinding to really get concepts, but the LMS only knew I'd accessed them once.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my main strategy for video content. I'd stream or download a lecture once, then watch it offline two or three more times while taking notes. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so downloading things when I could and then studying them later was essential for me. The LMS probably thought I barely engaged with videos when really I was wearing them out.
Q12: There was a rough patch mid-semester when my mom got sick and I had to pick up extra shifts at work to help with bills. My attendance dropped even more during that time, and I just couldn't bring myself to log in and face all the things piling up. It wasn't that I stopped caring—I was actually really stressed about falling behind—but I was physically and emotionally drained.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those things affected me. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about some topics made me avoid those modules entirely because I didn't know where to start asking for help. Having to balance work, family stuff, and school meant the LMS often got whatever scraps of energy I had left.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open while doing other things, or I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing them. Sometimes I was just too tired to focus, so I'd technically be 'studying' but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Those sessions felt performative, like I was going through the motions to feel less guilty about not engaging properly.
Q15: I definitely postponed the bigger assignments even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start, especially when I'd missed classes and felt lost. There was also this weird avoidance thing where the more behind I got, the scarier it felt to face the work, so I'd distract myself with smaller tasks instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine—they determined everything about when and how I used the LMS. I'd have these bursts of activity right before things were due, logging in multiple times a day to check requirements and submit work. Without a deadline looming, I struggled to motivate myself to engage proactively with the material, even though I knew I should.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging or showed me exactly what to fix, it motivated me to engage more with similar assignments. But honestly, feedback sometimes came too late for me to really use it on the next thing. The CBT sessions I had helped me process some of my reactions to negative feedback, which was useful because I used to spiral when I got low marks.
Q18: The course structure was a bit overwhelming with so many online modules—seventeen courses worth of content is a lot to juggle. I appreciated when things were broken into smaller chunks because that felt more manageable. But when modules were long and required sustained attention, I struggled because my study sessions had to be flexible and often interrupted.
Q19: Having clear, step-by-step instructions made engagement way easier for me. What made it harder was when resources assumed you had consistent internet access or lots of time to sit and read. I also found it difficult when there wasn't much opportunity for hands-on practice, since that's really how I learn best. More interactive elements would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard but struggling to understand the material well enough to complete assignments. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, searching for clarity, but still not feeling confident enough to submit work. It could also be someone dealing with anxiety about their performance, so they prepare a lot but freeze when it's time to turn something in.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat—my assignment scores were decent even though my LMS activity looked low. I think it's because a lot of my real learning happened offline or in ways the system couldn't track. Someone might understand the material well from external resources or have strong foundational knowledge, so they don't need to engage as much with every online component.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active and whether you're meeting deadlines, which is useful data. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just clicking through. They also can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or life circumstances that affect how someone shows up to their coursework.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and actively working to learn it, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present when you're studying and feeling connected to the content and the course community. Even when I struggled this semester, I still cared deeply about learning—I just couldn't always show it in traditional ways.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the discussions with classmates outside the platform, or the emotional effort it took to keep going when I was exhausted. It also wouldn't show that failing this course doesn't mean I wasn't engaged—it means I was dealing with a lot while trying my best with limited resources.
